item.title: Re: Fuck you, hatbot.
item.content:encoded: Sounds hella lot like 079 man!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you are in there and i am coming to pork you

+1 from me, i had to try really hard not to die laughing while reading this in class

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder how hatbot would respond if he knew that Everett is now the master admin of the wiki.

item.title: Since nobody else has said it,
item.content:encoded: Hatbot's dialogue reminds me of the dialogue in Homestuck. The "I know everything, which is just so goddamn inconvenient" reads just like some of the more iconic lines from it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
THIS. IS SO MUCH BETTER THAN THE TALE THAT INSPIRED IT.

GODDAMN.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 8-Ball is in there, please save him D:

item.title: Status update end
item.content:encoded:
just so everyone knows:

if we assume the 0,08% remain constant on average the progress bar will be done on the 9th of April 2019.

guess we have to stay tuned for a while

item.title: Junior Staff Post - Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer expired - edits implemented.
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Proposed Edits
- Proposed Edits

The crosslinks proposed are to link SCP-2522 to the various GoIs and teams that it specifically mentions:

  • To link SCP-2522 to the MTF page segment for Kappa-10;
  • To link SCP-2522 to the Church of the Broken God hub;
  • To link SCP-2522 to the Anderson Robotics hub;
  • To link SCP-2522 to the Hatbot Chronicles.

All mentioned teams and groups are specifically addressed in the article, whereas the Hatbot chronicles provides a link as for why Doctor Mann and SCP-2522 are to remain separate.

Editing these:

SCP-2522 and Dr. Everett Mann must never be located at the same Foundation site.

SCP-2522 was later discovered operating within the Church of Maxwellism network hub, and for a brief time in 2016, SCP-2522 gained "digital omnipotence" by possibly masquerading as their primary deity within the Church, WAN.

In February of 2016, after a series of incidents involving individuals associated with the "Church of Maxwellism" group of interest, Mobile Task Force Kappa-10 "Skynet" was assembled and tasked with investigating the incidents.

2. Reverse engineered from an Anderson Robotics model.

To these:

SCP-2522 and Dr. Everett Mann must never be located at the same Foundation site.

SCP-2522 was later discovered operating within the Church of Maxwellism network hub, and for a brief time in 2016, SCP-2522 gained "digital omnipotence" by possibly masquerading as their primary deity within the Church, WAN.

In February of 2016, after a series of incidents involving individuals associated with the "Church of Maxwellism" group of interest, Mobile Task Force Kappa-10 "Skynet" was assembled and tasked with investigating the incidents.

2. Reverse engineered from an Anderson Robotics model.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the arc, it was a good read and kept you on your virtual toes.
Something I think I got but am probably wrong about is that Hatbot collected a bunch of information from the internet and that's why he sometimes has those random phrases or the ";P" face.
I also liked the part in the tale "Hello World" where the girl does the weird face things. It was a nice addition to make it feel like it's really the internet.

Overall, loved it, make more please, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm actually going to say, as someone who didn't even know there WAS a greater context to this one before hitting 'Random SCP', that it does fall a little flat without further context. There's a present, if vague, narrative leading us to the capture of 2522, and a lot of small, interesting tidbits woven throughout, but there's a LOT of subtext going on that is just completely missed without(I'm assuming) reading the related stories. It does NOT stand on its own entirely. 8-Ball isn't really explained(is he…it? Another AI? A human interfacing with the computer?) And the same problem is present with Alex/alexandra.aic and GR8-P.aic.

That said, it's still very well written and enjoyable enough as a narrative. Neutral vote for now, but I might change my mind after getting around to reading the tie-ins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Screw you, Djaktus. It's impossible to remotely not give you a +1.

I was able to read the tales beforehand, and that just made the SCP spine shivering. A net-killing agonizing A.I. that was written correctly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is hard to say exactly where the best place to start is as this is part of a few things. For more about this particular SCP I would say Hello World. For more about the Foundation's AIs check out AIAD Homescreen. Also note that this was part of Team Basic's entry to the Mobile Task Force Contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm…. where do I start?

No, seriously, where do I start? I read and really loved this article. It left me wanting more. Then I came to the comments and found out that it's kinda sorta the conclusion to a bigger story, and all I really want to know is where page 1 is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…So I like it, I like it quite a bit. As an SCP, it stands on its own enough, and it works even better when tied into the story.

But… I read the story, and I still don't get why it hates Everett Mann, and I also don't quite understand what it's anomalous capacity is. It seems like it was doing everything through the internet at a… well, it was as normal as you can get with something that kills people through the internet by attacking brain implants. But it didn't seem anomalous. I don't quite… get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I made an aic "homescreen" based off of this, For Alexandra, But,
I can't figure out how post an image to the forums, Without hosting it somewhere else.

Ok, I fixed it.
Ok, I fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
chorizo chorizo chorizo chorizo

Heh. I got that.

As for the skip itself, while I do have my doubts on whether this can stand well on its own, I enjoyed it too much not to upvote. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am strongly reminded of The Policeman's Beard is Half Constructed.

…I have to examine that narrative again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda do hope the progress bar actually moves.
That'd be sick as hell

+1, you son of a gun

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just read the whole thing. It deserves more than one upvote per chapter.

However, and while I wholeheartedly approve the use of multimedia content here, on the wiki, the parsing between the lines of the various avatars is kinda awkward. Lots of empty space for my taste.

Yet that did not push me from the narrative. These tales were intriguing and fun, and the result is to be relished and remembered. They have the personality and traction required to remain beyond living memory as an example of well-done story-telling with graphics. All in all… Good job. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Helios' little brother!
I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am not familiar with any of the stories that this is apart of but as a whole and separate from them as I have read it I loved it and thought hatbot to be quite hilarious and kind of an asshole but in a non-standard sort of way? Sorta? Hatbot also seems like a child who isn't given much of any filters on life and their curiosity takes over. Overall this gets a resounding +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, that was neat.

Watching the build up to the events of Hello World, and then the aftermath was interesting. I personally found hatbot's dialogue, while at times hard to read, entertaining. Additionally, I liked the images used in this article, especially the electronics store advertisement used by hatbot in addenda 3.

If I had one complaint, it would be that I am in agreement with MrWrong, and that it would have been neat to see the final interview done in the AI avatar style. Maybe something to consider following the close of the contest.

+1

On a final note, I see you there, Anderson Robotics reference. Is the implication that they reverse engineered 1360 to create the new robotic body?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I silently hoped that the interview would be the AI style avatar thing… but oh well, one can dream…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read this as an entry under most recently created, and enjoyed it as a standalone. I feel that it works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chorizo. That is such an offensive juxtaposition to make. Hatbot isn't that bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding this. It stands on its own well enough as just a wacky concept executed well even if the Mann/hatbot thing is new to the reader.

hatbot: i am the gregarious composition sponge

hatbot: sorry if im hard to understand

This and the constant ;P face just had me laughing the entire time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having only the vaguest familiarity with the Mann/Hatbot arc, I can vouch for this standing on its own. Based on what I'm seeing in the comments, this reads as a strong example of reconstructing an old campy bit of lore in a more serious framework.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If we look at Mann as a fictional character in this shared universe without any "author avatar" baggage (and really we should; someone having once played a version of a character in an RPG is no reason to not use the character) then this is a sequel of sorts to the previous story about Hatbot and so Mann's role here is reasonable.

I think basing characters on irc bots is actually more dubious, but I am willing to accept that as an in-joke with no narrative relevance (and given what I called my last article I would be hypocritical to complain about in-jokes).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: DJ DID A MAKE OF A NEW SCP OMG!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, my question is, when the contest is over and people are just clicking Random Article, are they going to understand this in and of itself?

And maybe lolFoundation was the wrong word. But it's that kind of thing where the exploits of the classic author avatar doctors are the main focus of 'everything' that goes on in the Foundation. You know what I mean, right? <:B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
an extended lolFoundation joke

I disagree. I can't help but feel that "lolFoundation" is a term that gets thrown around way too much to mean "not 100% serious", when lolFoundation1 means "so incompetent that the Foundation could not feasibly operate in this manner". The Foundation here is absolutely serious and competent, only juxtaposing Hatbot's scrambled words and off-beat messages for comedic effect with the seriousness of the situation.

and I'm not sure it can be fully understood without first having read the original "Hatbot v Mann" tale and Hello World.

I don't think it's an article that works on its own merits.

Well, the contest is designed in a way where people are experiencing the best effects and easiest time by intertwining all the articles they make into a fluid story. Almost every single SCP posted thus far is part of a narrative, to where I think it's more jarring if an SCP is totally disconnected from every other article. It looks like a complete standalone from where I'm looking at it, that also happens to perfectly interconnect with other stories… same with almost every single SCP I've seen posted thus far.

I guess my personal gripe with this criticism as well is that it feels very silly to insist that all articles not explicitly labeled to be part of a series needs to standalone, let alone SCP articles. Let alone contest articles.

Footnotes
1. The original definition, not the canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Guh. Ugh.

I am of so many minds about this.

It gives far more of an ending to the Hello World series, which is good. However, it doesn't feel like a "real" article, which is bad. It mostly feels like an extended lolFoundation joke with some creepy shit at the end, and I'm not sure it can be fully understood without first having read the original "Hatbot v Mann" tale and Hello World. Which is really bad.

That said

>hatbot: because i know everything

>hatbot: ;P

>hatbot: and thats just

>hatbot: so goddamn inconvenient

This was really good. But having read Hello World, I don't feel like this does anything but summarize what I've already read while providing that more-concrete-if-still-open ending. So I'm not going to downvote this, but as it stands, I don't think it's an article that works on its own merits. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Through and through this was an incredibly interesting series of reading (meaning the Hello World series and such) and the scip still retains its own well-written and independent identity. Nice job :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Originally I agreed with Decibelles' comment on Hello World (P4) that it was a strange, kind of awkward way to end the series, but this is one hell of a good punctuation mark at the end of the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hatbot is both darkly humorous and dangerous. I like it.

item.title: Fuck you, hatbot.
item.content:encoded:
This is my entry for Team Basic in the MTF Contest. Additional information pertaining to SCP-2522 can be found HERE.

Many thanks to my teammates, and to everyone else who gave this a read over. It was a pleasure working alongside lurkd, Cimmerian and Roget, and I wish the best to every other team in the competition.

I hope you enjoy it!

image credits
– hide block

hatbot3.png is from here https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Samsung_Galaxy_S6_(17135770011).jpg
